coredata.dc:title: A magnetic thermosensitive fluorescence imprinted polymer for selective detection of bovine hemoglobin
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Lei
Zhang, Zhaohui
Xiao, Haimei
Chen, Shan
Fu, Jilin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a new type of magnetic thermosensitive fluorescence imprinted polymer was prepared for high selective identification of bovine hemoglobin with the magnetic carbon dots as the fluorescence source, poly(n-isopropylamide) and N, N-methylenebis acrylamide as the functional monomers and cross-linker, respectively. The magnetic thermosensitive fluorescence imprinted polymer was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared, fluorescence spectrometer, scanning electron microscope, transmission electron microscope and vibrating sample magnetometer in detail. The fluorescence of the magnetic thermosensitive fluorescence imprinted polymer was quenched linearly with bovine hemoglobin concentration in the range of 0.1- 9.0 μM with the detection limit of 48.0 nM. The imprinting factor was 2.3, which indicated excellent specificity of the magnetic thermosensitive fluorescence imprinted polymer toward bovine hemoglobin. With the advantage of the thermosensitivity, the elution efficiency of bovine hemoglobin could be significantly simplified and enhanced. In addition, the developed method was applied for selective identification of bovine hemoglobin in the real samples with the recoveries of 99.0–104.7%. This proposed protocol provides a feasible and reliable strategy for preparation of the smart imprinted material with multiple stimulation responses.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic
Fluorescence
Thermosensitive
Imprinting
Bovine hemoglobin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of silicon coated magnetic carbon dots (M-CDs@SiO2)
Influence of pH on the fluorescence response of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP.
Fluorescence intensity change of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP and M-CDs@SiO2@NIP with incubation time.
Influence of different pH values on the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence response of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP.
Stern-Volmer plots of M-CDS@SiO2@MIP and M-CDS@SiO2@NIP with BHb.
Selectivity of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP and M-CDs@SiO2@NIP toward BHb, BSA, HRP, OVA.
Determination of BHb in1000-fold dilution bovine blood (n = 3).
Performance comparison of different detection methods for BHb.

head.ce:title: A magnetic thermosensitive fluorescence imprinted polymer for selective detection of bovine hemoglobin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Mineral Cleaner Production and Exploit of Green Functional Materials in Hunan Province, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Mineral Cleaner Production and Exploit of Green Functional Materials in Hunan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cKey Laboratory of Mineral Cleaner Production and Exploit of Green Functional Materials in Hunan Province, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a new type of magnetic thermosensitive fluorescence imprinted polymer was prepared for high selective identification of bovine hemoglobin with the magnetic carbon dots as the fluorescence source, poly(n-isopropylamide) and N, N-methylenebis acrylamide as the functional monomers and cross-linker, respectively. The magnetic thermosensitive fluorescence imprinted polymer was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared, fluorescence spectrometer, scanning electron microscope, transmission electron microscope and vibrating sample magnetometer in detail. The fluorescence of the magnetic thermosensitive fluorescence imprinted polymer was quenched linearly with bovine hemoglobin concentration in the range of 0.1- 9.0 μM with the detection limit of 48.0 nM. The imprinting factor was 2.3, which indicated excellent specificity of the magnetic thermosensitive fluorescence imprinted polymer toward bovine hemoglobin. With the advantage of the thermosensitivity, the elution efficiency of bovine hemoglobin could be significantly simplified and enhanced. In addition, the developed method was applied for selective identification of bovine hemoglobin in the real samples with the recoveries of 99.0–104.7%. This proposed protocol provides a feasible and reliable strategy for preparation of the smart imprinted material with multiple stimulation responses.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, quantum dots (QDs) have attracted much attention for their unique optical properties including narrow emission spectra, large Stokes shift, size-dependent emission and great photostability [1]. Nevertheless, the toxicity of traditional QDs (e.g., CdSe, CdTe) limits their application to some extent. Carbon dots (CDs), as novel fluorescent materials, exhibited special advantages involving low toxicity, resistant to photobleaching, soluble, excitation dependence and high quantum yields [2,3], which have been gradually become substitute for traditional QDs [4]. Currently, many CDs-based probes have been developed for detect metal ion, small molecules and biomolecules [5,6]. However, the selectivity of CDs-based probes is still a challenging topic [7]. An effective way to improve the selectivity of CDs-based probes is the combination of molecular imprinting technology.

Molecular imprinting technology is an interesting way to synthetic a class of artificial receptor with tailor-designed recognition sites [8,9]. Typically, the preparation procedure of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) involves the copolymerization of functional monomers and cross-linkers in the presence of the template, and then the special tailor-made imprinted cavities with memory of template are exposed after removal of the template [[10-12]]. Recently, a number of CDs-based MIP which combined the merits of high sensitivity of fluorescence and selectivity of MIP has been reported. Zhou et al. prepared a new fluorescence approach for the determination of paranitrophenol based on eco-friendly CDs-based MIP composite [13]. Guo et al. developed CDs-based MIP for specific fluorescence detection of bisphenol A [14]. Even though CDs-based MIP has made some satisfactory achievements toward small molecules [15], the imprinting of protein is still great challenge due to the larger molecular weight, flexible structure, and mild living condition [16,17].

To overcome above problems, thermosensitive protein imprinted polymer has been proposed owing to its virtue of enhanced rebinding percentage and mass transfer [18,19]. For instance, Zhang's team synthesized thermo responsive fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymers for the controlled and sustained release of target protein under external stimulus [20]. Karfa's team proposed a multi-stimuli responsive fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymer for detection of alpha-fetoprotein [21]. Li's team developed a thermosensitive imprinted polymer with surface imprinting and epitope approach for specific recognition cytochrome C [22]. Our team has prepared magnetic fluorescence MIP using Fe3O4 modified CDs composited nanoparticle as the fluorescence source and supporting matrix for sensitive and selective detection of bovine hemoglobin (BHb) [23]. Up to date, smart imprinted polymers were mainly single-stimuli responsive, and although some multi-stimuli responsive imprinted polymer have been proposed which endure the drawback of the complexity of synthesis and time-consumed.

A multi-stimuli BHb imprinted polymer was developed with magnetic CDs as the fluorescence source and supporting substrate, N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) as the thermosensitive functional monomer in this paper. The multi-stimuli MIP showed high selectivity, thermosensitivity, fluorescence response and magnetic property. The MIP was used for detecting BHb in bovine blood samples successfully, which provides feasibility for the selective imprinted extraction and rapid selective recognition of proteins.

Triethylenetetramine (TETA, 70%) and ammonium ferric citrate (FAC) were purchased from Aladdin Agent (Shanghai, China). Bovine hemoglobin (BHb, MW 64.5 KDa, pI=6.9), bovine serum albumin (BSA, MW 66.0 kDa, pI=4.9), ovalbumin (OVA, MW 44.5 kDa. pI=4.5), and poly(n-isopropylamide) (NIPAM) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, MO). N, N-methylenebis acrylamide (MBA), ammonium persulfate (APS), and tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED) were purchased from Tianjin Kemio Chemical Reagent Co. LTD (Tianjin, China). Ammonium hydroxide (NH3•H2O, 25%), isopropanol, acetone, and tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Changsha Chemical Reagent Co. (Changsha, China). All regent were of analytical grade and used as received.

Fourier-transform infrared spectra were recorded on IRAffinisy-1 Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence was measured on an F-7000 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). The magnetic property of the polymers was characterized by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, M27407, Lake Shore Ltd.). The morphology was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss-Sigma HD, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, USA).

M-CDs were obtained according to the previously reported method with some modification [24]. In brief, 0.33 g of FAC was dissolved the mixture of 2.5 mL of TETA and 22.5 mL of deionized water. Then the precursor solution transferred to a 50-mL autoclave with Teflon-lined was heated at 200 °C for 6 h. Cooled to room temperature, the M-CDs were washed repeatedly with acetone three times to remove free M-CDs and excess TETA. Collected with the help of an external magnetic field, the M-CDs were dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 5 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of silicon coated magnetic carbon dots (M-CDs@SiO2)
ce:section.ce:para: M-CDs were encased with a silicon layer according to the modified Stöber method [25]. In brief, 0.05 g of M-CDs was dispersed in the mixture of 4.0 mL of deionized water with 10.0 mL of isopropanol, in which ammonium hydroxide was used to adjust the pH of the mixture to 9.0. After that, 0.50 mL of TEOS was added and the mixture was kept shocking for 12 h. The obtained M-CDs@SiO2 was washed by the deionized water repeatedly. Finally, M-CDs@SiO2 was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 5 h.

Firstly, 100.0 mg of M-CDs@SiO2 was dispersed in 30.0 mL of deionized water. Then 20.0 mg of BHb was added and the mixture was shaken for 30 min. The mixture was pre-polymerized by addition 200.0 mg of NIPAM and 80.0 mg of MBA with vibration for 1 h. After that, 20.0 mg of APS and 0.20 mL of TEMED were added in the pre-polymer solution under protect by N2 for 15 min to remove the oxygen. After shaken for 5 h, the product was washed repeatedly with 0.5% of triton X-100 at 20 °C until no BHb was detected in the eluent by UV–vis. The resultant M-CDs@SiO2@MIP was dried in vacuum drying oven at 55 °C for overnight.
The non-imprinted polymer based on M-CDs@SiO2 (M-CDs@SiO2@NIP) was obtained using the same step without the template.

All fluorescence detection was carried out with the same condition. The excitation wavelength was 360 nm and the range of emission wavelength was 400–650 nm. The slit width of excitation and emission were both 10 nm and the photomultiplier tube voltage was set at 650 V.

Bovine blood sample was obtained from a healthy bovine and then diluted 1000-fold through PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH =7.0), to demonstrate the applicability of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP for specific recognition and quantitative fluorescence detection of BHb as reported literatures [9,26]. As no BHb in the collected bovine blood sample was detectable by the method, the recovery was investigated by the standard addition method.

The synthetic route of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP was shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, the M-CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. Then, silicon layer was introduced onto the M-CDs surface using the modified Stöber method to improve the fluorescence and stability of the M-CDs [27]. Subsequently, amino and carboxyl of the template molecule pre-combined with NIPAM and MBA through hydrogen bond to form template-functional monomer complexes. After that, a thermosensitive imprinted layer was coated on the silica-coated M-CDs surface by interaction with crosslinking agent. Finally, the multi-stimuli M-CDs@SiO2@MIP with well-defined recognition ability toward BHb was obtained after removal of the template with 0.5% of triton X-100 at 20 °C.

Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared M-CDs, M-CDs@SiO2, M-CDs@SiO2@MIP and M-CDs@SiO2@NIP was compared. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of the M-CDs decreased gradually with modified by silicon and imprinted layer owing to the shielding effect. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP was restored remarkably to 92.5% of that of the M-CDs@SiO2@NIP after removal of template, which indicated that BHb was removed cleanly from the imprinted cavity cavities in the polymer matrix

As shown in Fig.S2, M-CDs showed the characteristic peaks at 569 cm−1, 1630 cm−1, and 3430 cm−1, which attributed to Fe-O, C = O, and OH stretching vibration, respectively. The data were consistent with the reported literature indicating the successful preparation of M-CDs [24]. The M-CDs@SiO2 appeared new characteristic peaks at 787 cm−1 and 958 cm−1 belonging to the vibration of Si-O group and Si-O-Si group, which indicated the silicon layer has been successfully coated the surface of M-CDs [28]. Compared with the M-CDs and M-CDs@SiO2, the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP had new characteristic peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 attributing to CH group vibration of NIPAM. In addition, the increase in adsorption intensity at 3430 cm−1 was attributed to the deformation vibration of NH of NIPAM [29]. All results demonstrated the MIP layer was successfully coated on the M-CDs@SiO2 surface.

The morphology of M-CDs, M-CDs@SiO2 and M-CDs@SiO2@MIP were characterized by TEM and SEM in detail. The SEM images of M-CDs, M-CDs@SiO2 and M-CDs@SiO2@MIP shown in Fig. S3 exhibited the uniform spherical particles. Moreover, the particles diameter increased significantly after coated with the layer of silica and MIP compared with the naked M-CDs. In addition, the TEM images of M-CDs in Fig. S3D showed the uniform and spherical nanostructure with the size of 20 nm. The M-CDs@SiO2@MIP shown in Fig. S3E displayed spherical particles with the diameters of 170 nm, which indicated that the M-CDs were successfully coated with the silica and MIP. The all results confirmed that the MIP was encased on the M-CDs successfully.

The magnetic properties of M-CDs, M-CDs@SiO2 and M-CDs@SiO2@MIP were characterized by VSM and the results were shown in Fig. S4. The saturation magnetization values of M-CDs, M-CDs@SiO2 and M-CDs@SiO2@MIP were 56.60, 46.30, and 42.00 emu/g, respectively. The gradually weakened tend of the magnetization intensity was ascribed to the shielding effect of the silicon layer and the imprinting layer toward the M-CDs. Fortunately, the magnetic intensity of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP is enough to separate from the solution rapidly under an external magnetic field within 2 min as displayed in the inset of Fig. S4.

The effect of incubation time on the fluorescence response of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP and M-CDs@SiO2@NIP toward the template was shown in Fig. 2 . After adding the template (5.0 μM), the fluorescence intensity decreased for both M-CDs@SiO2@MIP and M-CDs@SiO2@NIP. The fluorescence intensity of the M-CDs@SiO2@NIP was balanced at 9.0 min, while the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP was stable at 12.0 min. The fluorescence response balancing time of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP was longer than that of the M-CDs@SiO2@NIP, which was ascribed to the M-CDs@SiO2@NIP rebinding with the template only by nonspecific adsorption. Thus, 12.0 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time for the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP in the further experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH on the fluorescence response of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity and rebinding capacity of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP toward BHb in different pH values solution were investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 3 . The fluorescence intensity of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP was stable with the pH values ranged from 5.0 to 7.0. However, the fluorescence intensity decreased obviously when the solution pH was over 7.0, which because the silicon layer of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP will be ionized in alkaline environment [30]. Moreover, the maximum variation of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP fluorescence response for BHb appeared in the pH value of 7.0 since BHb may be denatured under acid and basic condition, which resulted in the reduction of the inter-reaction with NIPAM [31–32]. Therefore, the optimized solution pH for fluorescence detection BHb was selected as 7.0.

It is well known NIPAM as a thermosensitive monomer which has low critical solution temperature about 32 °C [33] can undergo a volume-phase transition with the temperature. To investigate the thermosensitivity character of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP, the temperature response study was performed. Fig. S5A shows the fluorescence response of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP toward BHb at 20 °C and 45 °C. At low temperature, the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP appeared high fluorescence intensity owing to NIPAM was swelled, which resulted in the template was released easily. On the contrary, at high temperature, NIPAM was shrunk and the template was tightly bounded which resulted in the fluorescence intensity of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP was weaken [28]. The results showed that the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP had conspicuous temperature dependent on-off fluorescence intensity, which indicated the template can control the release of by changing the external temperature.

Moreover, the elution efficiency of template was investigated with different conditions. As shown at Fig. S5B, the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP after removal of template with 0.5% of triton X-100 at 45 °C can reach 90.0% of the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs@SiO2@NIP. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP after removal of template with 0.5% of triton X-100 at 20 °C can reach more than 94% of the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs@SiO2@NIP, which indicated the elution efficiency of bovine hemoglobin was enhanced.

To study the specific recognition ability of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP, the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP and M-CDs@SiO2@NIP toward BHb with different concentrations ranged from 0.1 μM to 9.0 μM were investigated. The M-CDs@SiO2@MIP and M-CDs@SiO2@NIP appeared fluorescence quenching behavior in the presence of BHb, and the fluorescence intensity of both decreased gradually with the increment of BHb concentration. However, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP was higher than that of the M-CDs@SiO2@NIP, which was due to the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP recognition ability originated from tailor made specific recognition site. Both the fluorescence quenching of M-CDS@SiO2@MIP and M-CDS@SiO2@NIP follows the equation of Stern-Volmer [34–35].
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
[ C ]

Where F0, F are the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP when the template was absence and presence, respectively. KSV is the quenching constant of Stern–Volmer and [C] is the concentration of template. The imprinting factor (IF), which was defined as the ratio of KSV, MIP and KSV, NIP (the slope of the linear equation), was used to evaluate the specificity of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP. According to the data shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence quenching ratio of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP exhibited a linear relationship toward BHb concentration range of 0.1 μM ~ 9.0 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.99. The linear equation was F0/F = 0.35[C] + 0.96 with the KSV,MIP of 349,600 M − 1. While for M-CDs@SiO2@NIP, the linear equation between fluorescence quenching ratio and BHb was F0 /F = 0.15[C] + 1.14 with the KSV,NIP of 152,900 M − 1. Thus, the value of IF was calculated as 2.3, which indicated that the M-CDS@SiO2@MIP had specific adsorption toward BHb. The detection limit of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP toward BHb was calculated as 48.0 nM on following 3σ IUPAC criteria (3σ/S), where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration.

To investigate the selective recognition of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP toward BHb, BSA, HRP, and OVA were chosen as competitive proteins. Fig. 5 shows that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP toward the three competing proteins was smaller than that toward BHb. While the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the M-CDs@SiO2@NIP toward all proteins was similar. Besides, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the M-CDs@SiO2@NIP was much less than that of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP toward BHb. This indicated that the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP had good selective recognition toward BHb.

Meanwhile, the interferences metal ions for the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP were also investigated under the optimal conditions. As shown in Table 1 , the tested ions had few effects on the fluorescence intensity of the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP, which proved that the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP possessed with good anti-interference ability.

To evaluate the application of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP in real environment, fluorescence detection BHb in 1000-fold dilution of bovine blood and spiked with different BHb concentrations was conducted. As shown Table 2 , the recoveries of BHb were ranged from 99.0% to 104.7% in the real environment, which indicated the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP can be successfully applied for the detection of BHb in real samples.

The performance comparison with different systems of previous work for the determination of BHb was summarized in Table 3 . The developed system provided a wider linear range with lower detection limit compared to other previously reported methods [36–38]. Moreover, the system construction process was facile and rapid compared with other detection methods.

The repeatability and reproducibility experiment was performed using seven batches of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP prepared with the same procedure, and each batch was tested in triplicate. After five cycles of template adsorption-desorption were performed and the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs@SiO2@MIP remained 90.0% of the original M-CDs@SiO2@MIP fluorescence intensity with an RSD less than 2.5%, which indicated that the M-CDs@SiO2@MIP could achieve better repeatability and reproducibility.

A novel magnetic, thermosensitivite and fluorescence multi-stimulate MIP was prepared successfully with surface imprinting technique using M-CDs as the fluorescence and magnetic source, and NIPAM as the functional monomer. The developed M-CDs@SiO2@MIP showed specific recognition toward BHb with the detection limit of 48.0 nM. The M-CDs@SiO2@MIP could successfully detect BHb in bovine blood with the recoveries of 99.0%−104.7%. This work provides feasibility for the selective imprinted recognition of proteins.

Lei Cai: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Zhaohui Zhang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Haimei Xiao: Methodology, Visualization, Software. Shan Chen: Data curation. Jilin Fu: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All the authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 21565014), National Experimental Teaching demonstration Center of Chemistry of Jishou University (2018ZXCX25).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104603.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid determination of malachite green in water and fish using a fluorescent probe based on CdTe quantum dots coated with molecularly imprinted polymer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wu, Z.Z. Lin, H.P. Zhong, X.M. Chen, Z.Y. Huang, Rapid determination of malachite green in water and fish using a fluorescent probe based on CdTe quantum dots coated with molecularly imprinted polymer, Sens Actuators B239 (2017) 69–75.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemiluminescence of carbon dots under strong alkaline solutions: a novel insight into carbon dot optical properties

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An eco-friendly molecularly imprinted fluorescence composite material based on carbon dots for fluorescent detection of 4-nitrophenol

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dot cluster as an efficient “off–on” fluorescent probe to detect Au (III) and glutathione

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamer carbon nanodot sandwich used for fluorescent detection of protein

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and characterization of fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymers as sensor for highly sensitive detection of dibutyl phthalate from tap water samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhou, T. Li, W. Xu, W. Huang, N. Wang, W. Yang, Synthesis and characterization of fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymers as sensor for highly sensitive detection of dibutyl phthalate from tap water samples, Sens Actuators, B240 (2017) 1114–1122.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel hybrid structure silica/CdTe/molecularly imprinted polymer: synthesis, specific recognition, and quantitative fluorescence detection of bovine hemoglobin

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The emerging technique of molecular imprinting and its future impact on biotechnology

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Core-shell quantum dots coated with molecularly imprinted polymer for selective photoluminescence sensing of perfluorooctanoic acid

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel composite of graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer for fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol
